did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning method called a double-branch network (DBN) with Common Feature Extraction Block (CFEBlock) and FusionBlock to classify clinical skin lesions. The model was trained and evaluated on the CSLI dataset containing 3361 clinical dermatology images for six disease categories.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as 'TYPE Original Research' in the header. It presents a novel double-branch network (DBN) algorithm, constructs a new dataset (CSLI), and reports original experimental results including accuracy, precision, sensitivity, and AUC metrics on test sets, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Classification of clinical skin lesions with double-branch networks
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of classifying clinical skin lesions by proposing a double-branch network (DBN) that utilizes Common Feature Extraction Blocks and FusionBlocks to enhance feature extraction and fusion. The model was evaluated on a newly constructed dataset named CSLI, which comprises 3,361 clinical dermatology images across six disease categories, including actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, and malignant melanoma. The authors divided the CSLI dataset into training, validation, and test sets to perform comprehensive evaluations using metrics such as accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score, and AUC. Experimental results demonstrated that the DBN achieved strong overall performance on the test data, outperforming several classical skin classification models. However, the study noted limitations regarding class imbalance, particularly lower accuracy for malignant melanoma and confusion between basal cell carcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma due to sample size disparities. The authors concluded that while the DBN offers greater flexibility and accuracy for focusing on lesion regions, future work should address data imbalance and reduce computational requirements.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the six disease categories in the CSLI dataset with 730 total images (584 train, 73 val, 73 test).
One of the six disease categories in the CSLI dataset with 1,136 total images (908 train, 114 val, 114 test). Noted as having high sample size and visual similarity to SCC.
One of the six disease categories in the CSLI dataset with 170 total images (136 train, 17 val, 17 test). Identified as having low accuracy due to small sample size and confusion with nevus.
One of the six disease categories in the CSLI dataset with 391 total images (313 train, 39 val, 39 test). High sample size influenced model judgment of MEL.

One of the six disease categories in the CSLI dataset with 298 total images (238 train, 30 val, 30 test). Noted for poor prediction results due to high similarity to ACK and BCC and smaller sample size.
One of the six disease categories in the CSLI dataset with 636 total images (508 train, 64 val, 64 test).

all_task_types.target: Six discrete clinical skin lesion disease categories (actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, malignant melanoma, melanocytic nevus, squamous cell carcinoma, seborrheic keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'Classification of clinical skin lesions' using a double-branch network (DBN). The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (six disease classes) from clinical dermatology images. The output is described as a 'six-category confidence score' used for class prediction, evaluated via accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score, and AUC metrics on the CSLI and HAM10000 datasets.

datasets_used.name: CSLI (Clinical Skin Lesion Images)
datasets_used.description: A newly constructed dataset created by the authors by combining their collected clinical images with the publicly available PAD-UFES-20 dataset. It contains clear clinical skin images of six common skin disease categories. The dataset is available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

use.training: Used for training the double-branch network (DBN) model. Images were center-cropped to crop out lesion areas.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training.
use.testing: Used for testing the model's performance, evaluating metrics such as accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset of dermatological images collected using different smartphone devices, containing images with varying resolutions, sizes, and lighting conditions. It was combined with the authors' collected data to construct the CSLI dataset.

use.training: Its images were combined with the authors' collected dataset to form the CSLI dataset, which was then used for training.
use.validation: Its images were combined with the authors' collected dataset to form the CSLI dataset, which was then used for validation.
use.testing: Its images were combined with the authors' collected dataset to form the CSLI dataset, which was then used for testing.
use.other: Source component for the CSLI dataset.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To analyze prediction errors, specifically identifying confusion between Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC), and to evaluate performance for Malignant Melanoma (MEL) in the context of class imbalance.

statistical_methods_used.method: Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve and Area Under Curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the model's predictive performance across all cut-off values, particularly for classes with low sample sizes like MEL and SCC, to determine if poor accuracy was due to bias or model inability.

To verify the effectiveness of specific components (CFEBlock and FusionBlock) by comparing the full DBN model against variants with removed components (e.g., DBN Fusion, DBN Small25, DBN Large50) and other classical models (MobileNetV2, DenseNet121, ResNet50, EfficientNet variants).
To prevent overfitting and excessive local weights during training.

statistical_methods_used.method: Data Augmentation (Random Vertical and Horizontal Flips)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To address the problem of overfitting.
To divide the CSLI dataset into training, validation, and test sets for model training and evaluation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between models were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score, AUC) and visual analysis of ROC curves and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

ablation_study.notes: DBN Small25 achieved the best overall accuracy. DBN Small50 achieved the highest AUC (0.937). The paper notes that DBN Small25 has better overall indices for the CSLI dataset.

comparison_with_classical_models.notes: DBN Small25 outperformed other classical models in Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, Specificity, Balanced Accuracy, and F1 Score. It tied for highest AUC with EfficientNetb7 and MobileNetV2.

CSLI (Clinical Skin Lesion Images).notes: The model performed poorly on MEL and SCC due to small sample sizes and visual similarity to other classes. For MEL, low accuracy was attributed to class imbalance (high bias towards NEV) rather than poor model ability, as indicated by high AUC. For SCC, confusion with BCC was noted. DBN Small25 showed better overall indices than other models.

comparison_with_classical_models.notes: DBN Small25 outperformed other models in Sensitivity, Balanced Accuracy, F1 Score, and tied for highest AUC with DenseNet121. It had slightly lower Accuracy and Precision than DenseNet121 and EfficientNetb7.

comparison_with_other_algorithms.notes: DBN achieved the highest accuracy among the listed comparative algorithms.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; the paper notes that existing one-way networks cannot be easily modified to add complete ImageNet pre-training weights, implying the proposed DBN structure may not support standard ImageNet pre-training directly, though MobileNetV2 was used as the backbone example.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (images from PAD-UFES-20 had varying resolutions/sizes; CSLI images were center-cropped to remove background).

architecture_details.backbone: MobileNetV2 (used as an example backbone; the framework supports other feature extraction networks)
architecture_details.structure: Two-branch network consisting of an 'original network branch' and a 'fusion network branch'. Both branches have identical structures.
modules: CFEBlock (Common Feature Extraction Block): Extracts common features between adjacent layers of the original branch and fuses them with the fusion branch.
FusionBlock: Fuses feature maps from the original and fusion branches using learnable parameters (gamma1, gamma2) and either dot product or addition operations.

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (mentions L2 regularization for loss function supervision)

preprocessing_techniques: Rigorous data cleaning: eliminating blurred, duplicate, and low-quality images.
Sample selection: keeping only one image of the best quality for multiple images of lesions from the same patient.
Center cropping: cropping images to isolate lesion areas and remove background, ensuring uniform sample distribution and preventing small lesions from disappearing due to scaling.
Data augmentation: applying random vertical and horizontal flips to address overfitting.
Feature map scaling: pre-scaling smaller feature maps in the CFEBlock to handle inconsistent sizes of deep and shallow feature maps.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to image data during training to address the problem of overfitting.

conclusions: The proposed Double-Branch Network (DBN) with Common Feature Extraction Blocks (CFEBlock) and FusionBlocks effectively classifies clinical skin lesions, outperforming classical models like MobileNetV2, DenseNet121, ResNet50, and EfficientNet variants on the CSLI dataset.
DBN Small25 achieved the best overall performance on the CSLI test set with an accuracy of 79.52%, precision of 77.83%, sensitivity of 75.58%, and AUC of 0.934.
On the HAM10000 dataset, DBN Small25 achieved an accuracy of 91.58% and AUC of 0.985, outperforming other classical models in sensitivity, balanced accuracy, and F1-score.

The dual-branch structure enhances the network's ability to focus on lesion regions by allowing bidirectional information transfer between shallow and deep feature maps, providing greater flexibility and accuracy.

Performance was lower for Malignant Melanoma (MEL) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) due to class imbalance (small sample sizes) and high visual similarity to other classes (e.g., MEL to NEV, SCC to BCC).
For MEL, the low accuracy was attributed to sample bias (high bias towards the larger NEV class) rather than poor model ability, as indicated by a high AUC (0.976).
For SCC, confusion with Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) was the primary source of error, driven by the large sample size disparity (BCC had nearly 1/3 of the dataset) and visual similarities.
Center cropping images to isolate lesion areas improved model performance slightly compared to using uncropped images, ensuring more uniform sample distribution.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding data imbalance and suggests future work should address these issues and reduce computational requirements through model pruning and lightweight strategies.
important_remarks: The study relies on a newly constructed dataset (CSLI) combining the authors' collected images with the public PAD-UFES-20 dataset; the CSLI dataset is not publicly available but only provided upon reasonable request, limiting reproducibility.

Significant class imbalance exists in the CSLI dataset, particularly for Malignant Melanoma (170 samples) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (298 samples) compared to Basal Cell Carcinoma (1,136 samples), which biases the model towards larger classes.

The model exhibits high confusion between Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) due to visual similarity and the large sample size disparity, leading to poor sensitivity for SCC.
Low accuracy for Malignant Melanoma (MEL) is attributed to sample bias (confusion with the larger Melanocytic Nevus class) rather than poor model ability, as evidenced by a high AUC (0.976) for MEL.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the significance of performance differences between the proposed DBN and baseline models.

The DBN architecture has high computational requirements due to multiple feature extraction networks and matrix multiplication operations in the FusionBlock, posing deployment concerns for resource-constrained environments.

The study acknowledges that the model's performance on cancerous lesions is limited by the inherently small sample sizes of cancer patients in clinical datasets, suggesting future work should address data imbalance and model pruning.
